JuicyGrape's blog

Codeforces Round 943 (Div. 3) Editorial
By JuicyGrape, 23 months ago,

We thank everyone who participated in the round.

1968A - Maximize?

Author: JuicyGrape

Hint 1

Try to find the upper bound of gcd(x,y)+ygcd(x,y)+y.

Hint 2

gcd(x,y)+y=gcd(x−y,y)+ygcd(x,y)+y=gcd(x−y,y)+y.

Hint 3

gcd(x,y)+y≤xgcd(x,y)+y≤x. Try to find such yy, that gcd(x,y)+y=xgcd(x,y)+y=x?

Solution

1968A - Maximize?

The core idea is to find the upper bound of gcd(x,y)+ygcd(x,y)+y.

Let us look closer at the formula gcd(x,y)+ygcd(x,y)+y. It is a well-known fact that gcd(a,b)=gcd(a−b,b)gcd(a,b)=gcd(a−b,b) for a≥ba≥b. Applying it to our formula, we get gcd(x−y,y)+ygcd(x−y,y)+y.

Using the fact that gcd(x−y,y)≤x−ygcd(x−y,y)≤x−y, we get gcd(x−y,y)+y≤x−y+y=xgcd(x−y,y)+y≤x−y+y=x. Hence, gcd(x,y)+y≤xgcd(x,y)+y≤x for 1≤y<x1≤y<x.

For y=x−1y=x−1, we have gcd(x,x−1)+x−1=1+x−1=xgcd(x,x−1)+x−1=1+x−1=x, which is the maximal possible value.

Note that the constraints allow finding the optimal yy in O(x)O(x), while the above solution works in O(1)O(1).

Implementation
#include<iostream>
using namespace std;
int main(){
	int t;
	cin>>t;
	while(t--){
		int x;
		cin>>x;
		cout<<x-1<<"\n";
	}
}
Rate the problem
  • Didn't solve 61
  • Good task 478
  • Average task 50
  • Bad task 39

1968B - Prefiquence

Author: FBI

Hint 1

Try to check for every kk if the prefix of aa of length kk is a subsequence of bb.

Solution

1968B - Prefiquence

We will be solving this task using dynamic programming.

Let us define dpidpi as the maximal prefix of aa that is contained in b1,…,bib1,…,bi as a subsequence.

Then the transitions are as follows:

  • if bibi is equal to adpi−1+1adpi−1+1 then dpi=dpi−1+1dpi=dpi−1+1.
  • otherwise dpi=dpi−1dpi=dpi−1.

The answer is dpmdpm.

Implementation
#include<iostream>
#include<vector>
using namespace std;
int main(){
	int t;
	cin>>t;
	while(t--){
		int n,m;
		cin>>n>>m;
		vector<char>a(n+1),b(m+1);
		for(int i=1;i<=n;i++){
			cin>>a[i];
		}
		for(int i=1;i<=m;i++){
			cin>>b[i];
		}
		vector<int>dp(m+1);
		dp[1]=(a[1]==b[1]?1:0);
		for(int i=2;i<=m;i++){
			if(dp[i-1]!=n && b[i]==a[dp[i-1]+1]){
				dp[i]=dp[i-1]+1;
			}else{
				dp[i]=dp[i-1];
			}
		}
		cout<<dp[m]<<"\n";
	}
}
Rate the problem
  • Didn't solve 35
  • Good task 195
  • Average task 108
  • Bad task 34

1968C - Assembly via Remainders

Author: JuicyGrape

Hint 1

Why constraints are small?

Hint 2

Try to think about increacing a1,…,ana1,…,an.

Hint 3

((a+b)moda)=b((a+b)moda)=b for 0≤b<a0≤b<a.

Solution

1968C - Assembly via Remainders

Notice that ((a+b)moda)=b((a+b)moda)=b for 0≤b<a0≤b<a. So we may try to generate a sequence with b=xib=xi.

Let us take a1=1000a1=1000, because 10001000 is larger than any of xixi. Then, we can take aiai as ai−1+xiai−1+xi, since ((ai−1+xi)modai−1)=xi((ai−1+xi)modai−1)=xi will be hold.

The maximal value of aa will be at most 1000+500n1000+500n what is smaller than 109109.

Implementation
#include<iostream>
using namespace std;
int main(){
	int t;
	cin>>t;
	while(t--){
		int n;
		cin>>n;
		int S=1000;
		cout<<S<<" ";
		for(int i=2;i<=n;i++){
			int x;
			cin>>x;
			S+=x;
			cout<<S<<" ";
		}
		cout<<"\n";
	}
}
Rate the problem
  • Didn't solve 61
  • Good task 376
  • Average task 59
  • Bad task 36

1968D - Permutation Game

Author: FBI

Hint 1

Consider a cycle of the permutation.

Hint 2

When it is optimal to stay on the same place till the end of the game?

Solution

1968D - Permutation Game

Because pp is a permutation, it will be divided into cycles.

For every player it is optimal to move during the first min(n,k)min(n,k) turns.

We will answer the question by calculating maximal possible score for both players.

Let us define sumisumi and posiposi:

  • sum of values in the positions that occur during the first ii turns.
  • position in which player will stop if he decides to move ii times.
We will calculate them as follows:
  • sumi=sumi−1+aposisumi=sumi−1+aposi
  • posiposi=pposi−1pposi−1.

Now the maximal possible answer for one player is equal to:

ans=max0≤i≤min(n,k)(sumi+(k−i)aposi)ans=max0≤i≤min(n,k)(sumi+(k−i)aposi)

Now we will compare maximal possible answers for every player.

Implementation
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
long long score(vector<int>&p,vector<int>&a,int s,int k){
	int n=p.size();
	long long mx=0,cur=0;
	vector<bool>vis(n);
	while(!vis[s]&&k>0){
		vis[s]=1;
		mx=max(mx,cur+1ll*k*a[s]);
		cur+=a[s];
		k--;
		s=p[s];
	}
	return mx;
}
int main(){
	int t;
	cin>>t;
	while(t--){
		int n,k,s1,s2;
		cin>>n>>k>>s1>>s2;
		vector<int>p(n),a(n);
		for(auto&e:p){
			cin>>e;
			e--;
		}
		for(auto&e:a){
			cin>>e;
		}
		long long A=score(p,a,s1-1,k),B=score(p,a,s2-1,k);
		cout<<(A>B?"Bodya\n":A<B?"Sasha\n":"Draw\n");
	}
}
Rate the problem
  • Didn't solve 111
  • Good task 450
  • Average task 34
  • Bad task 23

1968E - Cells Arrangement

Author: JuicyGrape

Hint 1

What is the maximal possible size of HH?

Hint 2

Can you always get that size for n≥4n≥4?

Hint 3

Consider odd and even distances independently.

Solution

Let us find an interesting pattern for n≥4n≥4.

Pattern for n=4

Pattern for n=5

Can you generalize the pattern? We put n−2n−2 cells on the main diagonal. Then put two cells at (n−1,n)(n−1,n) and (n,n)(n,n).

But why does it work? Interesting fact, that in such way we generate all possible Manhattan distances. Odd distances are generated between cells from the main diagonal and (n−1,n)(n−1,n). Even distances are generated between cells from the main diagonal and (n,n)(n,n).

Implementation
#include<iostream>
using namespace std;
int main(){
	int t;
	cin>>t;
	while(t--){
		int n;
		cin>>n;
		for(int i=1;i<=n-2;i++){
			cout<<i<<' '<<i<<"\n";
		}
		cout<<n-1<<' '<<n<<"\n"<<n<<' '<<n<<"\n";
	}
}
Rate the problem
  • Didn't solve 108
  • Good task 303
  • Average task 34
  • Bad task 395

1968F - Equal XOR Segments

Author: JuicyGrape

Hint 1

Is it useful to divide the segment into more than three segments?

Hint 2

Use the fact, that x⊕x=0x⊕x=0 and x⊕x⊕x=xx⊕x⊕x=x.

Hint 3

When you can divide the segment into 22 parts?

Hint 4

When can you divide the segment into 33 parts?

Solution

1968F - Equal XOR Segments

Observation: any division on more than k>3k>3 segments can be reduced to at most 33 segments.

Proof: suppose the XORs of elements from the segments are x1,…,xkx1,…,xk. The condition x1=x2=⋯=xkx1=x2=⋯=xk must be fulfilled. If k>3k>3 we may take any three consecutive segments and merge them into one, because x⊕x⊕x=xx⊕x⊕x=x. In such way, we reduce kk by two. We may repeat this process while k>3k>3.

Let us construct an array b1=a1b1=a1 and bi=bi−1⊕aibi=bi−1⊕ai for i≥2i≥2. Now, al⊕al+1⊕⋯⊕ar=br⊕bl−1al⊕al+1⊕⋯⊕ar=br⊕bl−1.

There are two cases:

  • k=2k=2, meaning that we divide the segment [l,r][l,r] into two segments. Suppose the segments are [l,m][l,m] and [m+1,r][m+1,r]. In such case we must check if bm⊕bl−1=br⊕bmbm⊕bl−1=br⊕bm. This equality reduces to bl−1=brbl−1=br.
  • k=3k=3, meaning that we divide segment [l,r][l,r] into [l,s][l,s], [s+1,t][s+1,t] and [t+1,r][t+1,r]. We must check if bs⊕bl−1=bt⊕bsbs⊕bl−1=bt⊕bs and bs⊕bt=br⊕btbs⊕bt=br⊕bt. It is equivalent to check if bl−1=btbl−1=bt and bs=brbs=br. Also, s<ts<t must be fulfilled. Hence, we may find the largest t<rt<r that bt=bl−1bt=bl−1 and the minimal s≥ls≥l that bs=brbs=br. It can be done using binary search on the positions of a certain value.
Implementation
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
const int N=200005;
int a[N];
int main(){
	int t;
	cin>>t;
	while(t--){
		int n,q;
		cin>>n>>q;
		map<int,vector<int>>id;
		id[0].push_back(0);
		for(int i=1;i<=n;i++){
			cin>>a[i];
			a[i]^=a[i-1];
			id[a[i]].push_back(i);
		}
		while(q--){
			int l,r;
			cin>>l>>r;
			if(a[r]==a[l-1]){
				cout<<"YES\n";
				continue;
			}
			int pL=*--lower_bound(id[a[l-1]].begin(),id[a[l-1]].end(),r);
			int pR=*lower_bound(id[a[r]].begin(),id[a[r]].end(),l);
			cout<<(pL>pR?"YES\n":"NO\n");
		}
		if(t)cout << "\n";
	}
}
Rate the problem
  • Didn't solve 85
  • Good task 388
  • Average task 18
  • Bad task 15

1968G1 - Division + LCP (easy version)

Author: JuicyGrape

Hint 1

Binary search.

Hint 2

How to check if two strings are equal?

Solution

1968G1 - Division + LCP (easy version)

Consider FℓFℓ is a function which returns true iff it is possible to divide the string into at least kk segments with their LCP equal to ℓℓ. Notice that FℓFℓ implies Fℓ−1Fℓ−1 for ℓ>0ℓ>0. So we may find the maximal ℓℓ using binary search, which will be the answer for the problem.

How to compute FℓFℓ? Let us find Z-function z1,…,znz1,…,zn of the given string. Notice that in the division w1,…,wkw1,…,wk we have w1w1 as the prefix of the given string and all the strings have the common prefix with w1w1 of length ℓℓ. Notice that zpzp gives us the longest common prefix with w1w1 and a segment starting at pp. So if zp≥ℓzp≥ℓ we may take this segment into account. In such greedy approach we count the maximal number of segments we can divide our string into. If this number is at least kk, then FℓFℓ is true.

The complexity is O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n).

Implementation
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
vector<int>Zfunc(string& str){
	int n=str.size();
	vector<int>z(n);
	int l=0,r=0;
	for(int i=1;i<n;i++){
		if(i<=r){
			z[i]=min(r-i+1,z[i-l]);
		}
		while(i+z[i]<n&&str[z[i]]==str[i+z[i]]){
			z[i]++;
		}
		if(i+z[i]-1>r){
			l=i;
			r=i+z[i]-1;
		}
	}
	return z;
}
int f(vector<int>&z,int len){
	int n=z.size();
	int cnt=1;
	for(int i=len;i<n;){
		if(z[i]>=len){
			cnt++;
			i+=len;
		}else{
			i++;
		}
	}
	return cnt;
}
int main(){
	int t;
	cin>>t;
	while(t--){
		int n,k;
		string s;
		cin>>n>>k>>k>>s;
		vector<int>z=Zfunc(s);
		int l=0,r=n+1;
		while(r-l>1){
			int mid=(l+r)/2;
			if(f(z,mid)>=k){
				l=mid;
			}else{
				r=mid;
			}
		}
		cout<<l<<"\n";
	}
}
Rate the problem
  • Didn't solve 32
  • Good task 142
  • Average task 29
  • Bad task 8

1968G2 - Division + LCP (hard version)

Author: JuicyGrape

Hint 1

Consider the general case: L=1L=1 and R=nR=n.

Hint 2

You must find the answer for each kk in the range [1,n][1,n]. Consider the cases k≤n−−√k≤n and k≥n−−√k≥n.

Solution

1968G2 - Division + LCP (hard version)

In general, in this version we must answer for each k∈{1,2,…,n}k∈{1,2,…,n}. We will use a similar idea as in the easy version considering two cases:

  • k≤n−−√k≤n. We may calculate these values as in the easy version in O(nn−−√logn)O(nnlog⁡n).
  • k>n−−√k>n. As we divide the string into kk segments and the LCP is ℓℓ, then k⋅ℓ≤nk⋅ℓ≤n. We get, that ℓ≤n−−√ℓ≤n. We may find the maximal possible kk for a fixed ℓℓ as our answer. It works in O(nn−−√)O(nn).

There are other approaches to solving this problem, but the authors believe this solution is the simplest one.

Implementation
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
vector<int>Zfunc(string& str){
	int n=str.size();
	vector<int>z(n);
	int l=0,r=0;
	for(int i=1;i<n;i++){
		if(i<=r){
			z[i]=min(r-i+1,z[i-l]);
		}
		while(i+z[i]<n&&str[z[i]]==str[i+z[i]]){
			z[i]++;
		}
		if(i+z[i]-1>r){
			l=i;
			r=i+z[i]-1;
		}
	}
	return z;
}
int f(vector<int>&z,int len){
	int n=z.size();
	int cnt=1;
	for(int i=len;i<n;){
		if(z[i]>=len){
			cnt++;
			i+=len;
		}else{
			i++;
		}
	}
	return cnt;
}
int main(){
	int t;
	cin>>t;
	while(t--){
		int n,L,R;
		string s;
		cin>>n>>L>>R>>s;
		vector<int>z=Zfunc(s);
		const int E=ceil(sqrt(n));
		vector<int>ans(n+1);
		for(int k=1;k<=E;k++){
			int l=0,r=n+1;
			while(r-l>1){
				int mid=(l+r)/2;
				if(f(z,mid)>=k){
					l=mid;
				}else{
					r=mid;
				}
			}
			ans[k]=l;
		}
		for(int len=1;len<=E;len++){
			int k=1;
			for(int i=len;i<n;){
				if(z[i]>=len){
					k++;
					i+=len;
				}else{
					i++;
				}
			}
			ans[k]=max(ans[k],len);
		}
		for(int i=n-1;i>=1;i--){
			ans[i]=max(ans[i],ans[i+1]);
		}
		for(int i=L;i<=R;i++){
			cout<<ans[i]<<' ';
		}
		cout<<"\n";
	}
}

      You don't need any of that. You could simulate what you do in G1 with a set/segment tree by iterating over the lengths, and it works. The time complexity came from harmonic series and the log from DS.

      23 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      Not sure what “parallel binary search” means, but it is nested binary search. I don't sort anything.

      Basically, we still have our nice zz function, however, we will also store an RMQ which can get the maximum value of zizi for all ii in a range [l,r][l,r].

      Let's precompute the value mxi=maximum number of disjoint substrings with length i,which are all equal to the prefix of length imxi=maximum number of disjoint substrings with length i,which are all equal to the prefix of length i

      If we want to check if kk disjoint substrings are possible with LCPLCP ll, we just check if mxl≥kmxl≥k.

      Now the problem is the computation of mxmx.

      For length ii, there can be at most nini disjoint substrings with that length. Let's use the fact that ∑ni=1ni=O(nlogn)∑i=1nni=O(nlog⁡n).

      Using our RMQ, we can, instead of calculating mximxi in linear time in the length, we can calculate it in O(mxilogn)O(mxilog⁡n), simply binary search on the next jj such that zj≥izj≥i (here, we binary search and use a sparse table to get this next jj in O(logn)O(log⁡n), alternatively, you can walk on a segment tree in O(logn)O(log⁡n)).

      The complexity of this computation is ∑ni=1mxi⋅logn=logn∑ni=1mxi≤logn∑ni=1ni=O(nlog2n)∑i=1nmxi⋅log⁡n=log⁡n∑i=1nmxi≤log⁡n∑i=1nni=O(nlog2⁡n)

      → Reply

        »

For B: Optimise Answer 

void solve() {
    int n, m; cin >> n >> m;
    string a, b; cin >> a >> b;
    
    int j = 0;
    for(int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        if(a[j] == b[i]) j++; 
    }

    cout << j << endl;
}

      Count number of non-overlapping occurrences of prefix of length lcp in s. Then binary search lcp, then cache it, then find ans for every k in [l..r][l..r].

Is this solution for G2 hackable? Using two heurestics:

1) Memoising the value of cnt calculated in f(mid)

2) Since the answer always decreases on increasing k, keeping the value of hi from previous k(not setting it to n again).

Not able to proove it's time complexity.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    ismailfateen
    23 months ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    ismailfateen
    23 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +27

    I have done the same thing, and I was able to prove the complexity to some extent. It's easy to see that my code is O(n⋅number of distinct values of mid)O(n⋅number of distinct values of mid).

    First let's assume l=1l=1 and r=nr=n, the binary search ranges are [1,n],[1,n2],…,[1,nn][1,n],[1,n2],…,[1,nn].

    The main observation is this, the range (n2,n](n2,n] is exposed to 11 binary search, the range (n3,n2](n3,n2] is exposed to 22 binary searches. Similarly, the range (ni,ni−1](ni,ni−1] is exposed to i−1i−1 binary searches.

    It is clear that with this, we have a very loose upper bound of O(n⋅∑ni=2min((i−1)⋅log(n),ni−1−ni)O(n⋅∑i=2nmin((i−1)⋅log⁡(n),ni−1−ni). However, as i said, this is very loose, as the sum ∑ni=2(i−1)⋅log(n)∑i=2n(i−1)⋅log⁡(n) is quite a bit more than n⋅log(n)n⋅log⁡(n) (the total number of checks).

    We can make this tighter.

    Let's consider the ranges 11 by 11, first we have the range (n2,n](n2,n]. We have 22 cases, the first case is that log(n)log⁡(n) is smaller than the range, in which case (for the sake of an upper bound), we should assume that we take all the log(n)log⁡(n) checks from that range, becuase we will never see that range again, so it is best to take as much as we can from that range, hence decreasing the number of times the other ranges can be chosen by log(n)log⁡(n).

    However, if the range length is less than log(n)log⁡(n), then all the next ranges are also smaller than log(n)log⁡(n). So in fact, a better upper bound is O(n⋅∑ni=2min(log(n),ni−1−ni))O(n⋅∑i=2nmin(log⁡(n),ni−1−ni)), this value is 733800000≈7×108733800000≈7×108 for n=2⋅105n=2⋅105.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      el_tunel
      23 months ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      el_tunel
      23 months ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 2 →  
      +8

      The point where the minimum changes from lgnlg⁡n to n/(i−1)−n/in/(i−1)−n/i will be i≈i0=n/lgn−−−−−−√i≈i0=n/lg⁡n, so this analysis gives a runtime bound proportional to around n⋅((n/i0)+(lgn)⋅i0)=2nnlgn−−−−−√n⋅((n/i0)+(lg⁡n)⋅i0)=2nnlg⁡n.

      → Reply
»
Noobish_Monk
23 months ago, show (+3) #

Noobish_Monk
23 months ago, hide # |
 
+8

For G2 there is also solution using the problem:

-we can deactivate an element

-we need to find the closest active element to the right

That can be done using dsu

Then we'll increase lcp. When we increase it by one, we deactivate elements, so we're left only with elements with zi≥curLCPzi≥curLCP where zizi is z-function.

To find maximum kk that can achieve at least curLCPcurLCP we will start at the beginning and then jump to the next active element of i+curLCPi+curLCP. Since the answer for curLCPcurLCP is at most ncurLCPncurLCP, O(ans)O(ans) solution works in O(nlogn)O(nlogn), and dsu gives us either α(n)α(n) or lognlogn

→ Reply

        23 months ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        In my code, the calc function is pretty much the same as the check function in your code. The got variable in your code stores the maximal k such that the answer is >= len. In my code, the calc function returns the got variable. Now I make an array called ans where ans[i] = maximal k such that the answer is >= i using this function. For answering queries I just binary search over this answer array.

        → Reply

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    vixxa
    23 months ago, show (+5) #
    »
    »
    vixxa
    23 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    I used hashing too, but I wasn't hacked since I used mersenne twister to randomly select mod for hashing.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      cseshamim47
      23 months ago, show (+4) #
      »
      »
      »
      cseshamim47
      23 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      Can you share your hashing template?

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        vixxa
        23 months ago, show (+3) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        vixxa
        23 months ago, hide # ^ |
         
        +4 
        mt19937 rng(chrono::steady_clock::now().time_since_epoch().count());
 
struct Hash {
    int n;
    string s;
    const ll p = 31;
    ll mod = uniform_int_distribution<int>((int)(1e9+7), (int)(1e9+1e8))(rng);
    vector<ll> pw, hash;
 
    Hash(string _s) : s(_s) {
        n = s.size();
        pw.resize(n+1);
        hash.resize(n+1);
        pw[0] = 1;
        for(int i=1; i<=n; i++) pw[i] = (pw[i-1] * p) % mod;
        hash[0] = s[0] - 'a' + 1;
        for(int i=1; i<n; i++) hash[i] = (hash[i-1] + pw[i] * (s[i] - 'a' + 1)) % mod;
    }
 
    ll getHash(int l, int r) { return (hash[r] - (l ? hash[l-1] : 0) + mod) % mod; }
 
    bool equal(int l1, int r1, int l2, int r2) {
        if(r1 - l1 != r2 - l2) return 0;
        if(l1 > l2) swap(l1, l2), swap(r1, r2);
        return (pw[l2-l1] * getHash(l1, r1) % mod) == getHash(l2, r2);
    }

    bool isPal(int l, int r) {
        return equal(l, r, n-1-r, n-1-l);
    }
};

        Here you go.

        Also be aware that the function isPal only works if the to the string is appended its reverse.

        For example, let's say we will call isPal for the string s = "cabad".

        To chech is substring from "aba" is palindrome, you have to initialize the hashing structure with the string "cabaddabac"

        → Reply

          »
          »
          »

          cseshamim47
          23 months ago, hide # ^ |
           
          0

          Does this line returns a prime number?

          ll mod = uniform_int_distribution<int>((int)(1e9+7), (int)(1e9+1e8))(rng);

          → Reply

            23 months ago, show (+1) #

            »
            »
            »
            »
            vixxa
            23 months ago, hide # ^ |
             
            +1

            No. It return random integer in the range [109+7,1.1∗109][109+7,1.1∗109].

            The mod doesn't need to be a prime number.

Hey,

  1. G2 is solvable in O(NlogNlogN): checking the maximum number of intervals one can separate in such that the common prefix has length at least k is doable in O(N/k logN) if we store the valid indices with Z[i]>=k in a set.

  2. Can someone understand why the solution to F gives TLE if we use unordered_map<int,vector > instead of map<int,vector >? My solution using unordered_map was TLEd in the main tests, and the official solution has the same issue when we use unordered_map. This seems unintuitive as we never use the key ordering.

Thanks!

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    AkiLotus
    23 months ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    AkiLotus
    23 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +8

    For the 2nd point, I think even from the main tests have some exploits on unordered_map to cause enough hash collisions for O(n)O(n) access. It's been a common exploit for quite a while, and it's due to hash table's nature itself, as long as its hashing could be reverse engineered.

    → Reply

why do we usually use a segment tree for finding xor on segments and not just how it was described in the problem f? or we just do this only when elements don't change?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    omsincoconut
    23 months ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    omsincoconut
    23 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Correct. We can do prefix XOR to evaluate range XOR in case that there are no updates (That is, no change). But if there are updates, then we use a Fenwick or segment tree.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      polsage
      23 months ago, show (+2) #
      »
      »
      »
      polsage
      23 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      how can we use segment tree pls tell??

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        omsincoconut
        23 months ago, show (+1) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        omsincoconut
        23 months ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        In the same way how it is used for sums, but you change each node to store the XOR of the interval instead.

        → Reply

I think there's an error in the editorial for problem F. It says "we may find the largest t <= r" but it should be t < r since if t == r then the segment [t+1, r] would not make sense. I tried a solution with this change in mind and it got AC. 259369531

problem E as per the tutorial: Interesting fact, that in such way we generate all possible Manhattan distances. Odd distances are generated between cells from the main diagonal and (n−1,n). Even distances are generated between cells from the main diagonal and (n,n)

but I guess if total x manhattan distances exist then we only get x-1 , how to prove we can get max x-1 distances only ?

if I am wrong please correct me

In problem F, what is wrong in the following logic: (Here a represents the prefix xor array and mp is mp[xor] = vector)

while(q--){
   int l,r; cin>>l>>r;
   if(a[r] == a[l-1]){
        cout<<"YES"<<endl;continue;
    }

    int x = *lower_bound(mp[a[r]].begin(), mp[a[r]].end(), l);
    int y = *lower_bound(mp[0].begin(), mp[0].end(), x+1);


    if(y<r && y>x && x>=l)cout<<"YES"<<endl; else cout<<"NO"<<endl;
   }
→ Reply
  • »
    »
    el_tunel
    23 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    el_tunel
    23 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    1. You need to look for the sum a[l - 1], not 0.

    2. The result of the second call to lower_bound() may not be dereferenceable.

    3. You don't have to check whether y>xy>x or x≥lx≥l, since these things are automatically true.

    → Reply
»
husain2088
23 months ago, show (+2) #
»
husain2088
23 months ago, hide # |
 
0

Why is O(nlogn) approach exceeding time limit in B question? Normally O(nlogn) works with n<2x10^5 and time limit = 2 sec.

(i tried to write a solution using upper bound 259169919)

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    AkiLotus
    23 months ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    AkiLotus
    23 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    You are using upper_bound incorrectly: the way set is structured isn't linear, thus it will make your command O(n)O(n).

    Instead, use the class method built for set, something like this:

    auto x1 = ons.upper_bound(lst);

    auto x2 = zrs.upper_bound(lst);

    → Reply

Can someone please explain to me the solution for problem D cause, I can't understand the editorial clearly

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    ishrak26
    23 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    ishrak26
    23 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Let me explain my idea:

    Let fs(p)fs(p) be the total score of a player when he starts at the position ss, always moves from his current position until he reaches pp, and after reaching pp, always stays at his current position. Bodya and Sasha's maximum score will be max1≤i≤nfPB(i)max1≤i≤nfPB(i) and max1≤i≤nfPS(i)max1≤i≤nfPS(i) respectively.

    This claim is correct because, a player will always move from his current position only when there lies some better position with higher axax, somewhere in the future along the path of his kk moves. Now we will prove why it is always better for him to move now instead of staying at the current position for some turns and then move.

    If his current position is xx, and he decides to stay at xx for some (one or more) turns, and then move to pxpx, there are the following three cases:

    1. ax<apxax<apx: It is obviously better to move to pxpx immediately and not waste turns at xx with lower score.
    2. ax=apxax=apx: Staying and moving doesn't really differ here. So there is no loss in moving.
    3. ax>apxax>apx: When he moves after some turns, he moves definitely in the hope of reaching some position yy from pxpx (through one or several moves in between) where ax<ayax<ay. So it is always better to spend these turns at the position yy than wasting them on xx.

    So the optimal move for a player will be — some number of moves (possibly zero), followed by some number of stays (possibly zero), until he completes his kk turns. So we basically iterate over his final position to know the maximum possible score.

    For all 1≤i≤n1≤i≤n, fs(i)fs(i) can be calculated in O(n)O(n) by keeping track of how many turns it takes to reach ii starting at ss and the total score obtained along the path.

    So overall running time of the solution is O(n)O(n).

    You can check my submission for better understanding: 259185280.

    → Reply
»

G2 卡常 厳しすぎ！！ (Constant factor for G2 too tight!)

I implemented the solution described in the editorial, with KMP instead of Z-function, but all my submissions ended up TLE41 (261016696) and I couldn't get the log^2 solution right.

By dividing the submission into >n−−√>n and ≤n−−√≤n parts I found my solution would finish in 3.2 seconds and I tried my best to squeeze in but still couldn't.

Could anyone help me optimize the constant factor further, or is KMP unable to pass?

BTW I don't think a 2e52e5 data range for a O(nn−−√logn)O(nnlog⁡n) intended solution is quite appropriate.

→ Reply

    23 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    if we divide the first part into < root(N / log(N)) and > root(N * log(N)) we can have a time complexity of n * root(n * log(n)). Maybe you can try this.

AksLolCoding
18 months ago, show #
»
AksLolCoding
18 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 3 →  
+1

G2 can be easily solved with z-function in O(nlogn^2). We find the maximal k for each answer i in increasing order. Use ordered set to maintain list of all indices in z-function that are greater than or equal to i and binary search for each next index. This allows us to binary search for each answer. Code

→ Reply

    First of all it is not possible to simulate all the moves since the constraints on number of moves is large . since each position in array 'A is associated with another position we need to move at max , min (n,k) times then for each move we can calculate the max score possible for the each player and after comparing maximum score achievable by both we can get the answer 359327583
